title: Addison's Disease
oneLiner: Primary adrenal insufficiency; autoimmune destruction most common; causes cortisol & aldosterone deficiency; life-threatening if crisis missed.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Adrenal crisis (hypovolaemic shock, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia)**  
  *Single finding:* Hyperpigmentation + postural hypotension + U&Es showing Na < 130, K > 5.5 in a shocked patient.
- **Sepsis (precipitant or mimic of crisis)**  
  *Single finding:* Fever + raised CRP/procalcitonin – but if in shock with pigmentation, treat for crisis first.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Gradual onset fatigue, weakness, weight loss  
  - Anorexia, nausea, vomiting, diffuse abdominal pain  
  - Craving salt  
  - Dizziness on standing, syncope  
  - Reduced libido, loss of body hair (women)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Hyperpigmentation (buccal mucosa, palmar creases, scars, extensor surfaces)  
  - Postural hypotension (systolic drop ≥20 mmHg)  
  - Signs of volume depletion (tachycardia, dry mucous membranes)  
  - Rarely: vitiligo, goitre (associated autoimmune disease)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Other causes of hyponatraemia** (SIADH, hypothyroidism, heart failure) – no hyperkalaemia or pigmentation  
- **Secondary adrenal insufficiency** (pituitary disease, glucocorticoid withdrawal) – no hyperpigmentation, normal aldosterone (renin normal), Na may be low but K normal  
- **Chronic fatigue syndrome / depression** – no electrolyte abnormalities, normal cortisol  
- **Anorexia nervosa** – no hyperpigmentation, low BP but hyperkalaemia rare  
- **Acute abdomen (e.g. perforation, pancreatitis)** – localised tenderness, no pigmentation, imaging + metyrapone test negative  
- **Sepsis alone** – inflammatory markers high, but U&Es usually normal unless AKI  
- **Splitter:** Hyperkalaemia + hyponatraemia + hyperpigmentation → primary Addison’s, not secondary
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - Lying/standing BP (postural drop)  
  - ECG: look for hyperkalaemia (peaked T waves, widened QRS)  
  - Capillary glucose: hypoglycaemia common  
- **Bloods:**  
  - U&Es, glucose, cortisol (random – low < 100 nmol/L highly suggestive; if > 500, excludes)  
  - ACTH (elevated in primary)  
  - Renin / aldosterone (low aldosterone, high renin)  
  - Autoantibodies (21-hydroxylase antibodies)  
  - FBC, CRP, blood cultures (if sepsis suspected)  
  - Thyroid function, coeliac screen (associated autoimmune)  
- **Imaging:**  
  - CT adrenals (to exclude haemorrhage, metastases, TB) if no clear autoimmune cause  
  - MRI pituitary if secondary suspected  
- **Special tests:**  
  - **Short Synacthen test** – give 250 mcg tetracosactide IV; measure cortisol at 0 and 30 min.  
  - **Diagnostic criteria:** Peak cortisol < 550 nmol/L (or < 500 depending on lab) = adrenal insufficiency  
- **Never-miss test:**  
  - **Blood for cortisol and ACTH BEFORE giving hydrocortisone if patient stable enough** – but if critically ill (shock), treat immediately and send a baseline cortisol sample (result may still be helpful). Do NOT withhold steroids for test.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If adrenal crisis (shock, severe hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia):  
    - IV hydrocortisone 100 mg bolus stat  
    - IV 0.9% saline 1 L over 30-60 min, repeat as needed (total up to 2-3 L in first 2-3 hours)  
    - Dextrose 50 mL of 50% (25 g) IV if hypoglycaemic  
    - Correct hyperkalaemia cautiously (rehydration + steroids usually enough; avoid insulin unless ECG changes)  
  - Treat underlying cause (e.g. antibiotics for sepsis)  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Continue IV hydrocortisone 100 mg IV q6h (or 50 mg q6h) for 24-72 h, tapering as clinical condition improves  
  - Maintain IV 0.9% saline with 5% dextrose if needed, guided by U&Es and fluid balance  
  - Monitor: NEWS2 hourly initially, U&Es + glucose 6-hourly, strict fluid balance  
  - Transition to oral when tolerating diet and haemodynamically stable  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Oral hydrocortisone: typical total 15-25 mg daily in divided doses (e.g. 10 mg on waking, 5 mg early afternoon, 5 mg early evening) – *check local policy*  
  - Fludrocortisone: 50-200 mcg once daily (mineralocorticoid replacement) – start 100 mcg, adjust based on U&Es and postural BP  
  - Sick day rules: double or triple oral hydrocortisone dose during illness; provide IM/SC hydrocortisone emergency kit for vomiting  
  - MedicAlert bracelet, emergency card, annual endocrinology review  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline ± 5% dextrose, rate based on U&Es – aim for euvolemia  
  - VTE prophylaxis: has been on steroids? Consider LMWH if no contraindications  
  - Diet: normal, encourage oral intake; if NBM for procedure, ensure IV hydrocortisone cover  
  - Observations: NEWS2, lying/standing BP, daily weights, U&Es daily until stable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**  
  - Hydrocortisone (IV): 100 mg bolus, then 100 mg q6h (taper as per local protocol)  
  - Hydrocortisone (oral): 15-25 mg total daily in 3 divided doses  
  - Fludrocortisone (oral): 50-200 mcg once daily  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Prednisolone (oral) 3-5 mg once daily – less preferred for mineralocorticoid effect  
  - Dexamethasone (IV/IM) if hydrocortisone unavailable (do not use for short Synacthen)  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Enzyme inducers (rifampicin, phenytoin, carbamazepine) accelerate hydrocortisone metabolism – increase dose  
  - Caution in peptic ulcer disease, diabetes, osteoporosis  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - No major DILI; adjust fludrocortisone if renal impairment (salt/water retention risk)  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Failing to give IV hydrocortisone in suspected adrenal crisis** – do not wait for test results.  
  - Missed stress dosing during intercurrent illness (e.g. surgery, infection) → precipitates crisis.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Adrenal crisis (hypotension, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia, cardiac arrhythmia)  
  - Vomiting → inability to take oral steroids → crisis cycle  
  - Hyperkalaemia-induced bradycardia or arrest  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Osteoporosis (long-term supraphysiologic steroids) – DXA scan if ongoing treatment years  
  - Autoimmune comorbidities: hypothyroidism, vitiligo, type 1 diabetes, pernicious anaemia, coeliac disease  
  - Adrenal crises with stress (50% of patients will have at least one crisis in 10 years)
sections.Prognosis:
- Near-normal life expectancy with appropriate replacement and sick-day management  
- Mortality from adrenal crisis is ~6-15% per crisis episode if untreated; most deaths preventable with education and emergency kits
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Give a MedicAlert bracelet and emergency card** – patient must carry injectable hydrocortisone and written crisis plan.  
- **Check for other autoimmune diseases** – screen thyroid function, coeliac serology, and perform annual review for vitiligo, diabetes.  
- **Teach sick day rules explicitly** – double or triple oral dose for fever, vomiting, infection; go to ED if vomiting persists.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not delay steroids for test results** if patient is hypotensive or has typical biochemistry.  
- **Do not stop steroids abruptly** – taper over days when switching from IV to oral.  
- **Do not underestimate stress dose requirements** – cover procedures (minor surgery: 50-100 mg IV, major: 100 mg q6h) – *check local policy for dosing.*
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Vomiting or diarrhoea with inability to take oral steroids  
  - Severe weakness, dizziness, abdominal pain, confusion  
  - Any injury, surgery, or fever ≥38°C without contact with endocrinology team  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Endocrinology clinic within 2-4 weeks of new diagnosis; routine annual review  
  - GP for monitoring of U&Es, blood pressure, BP lying/standing
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU / crash team if:**  
  - Systolic BP < 90 mmHg despite 2 L IV fluid challenge  
  - Glasgow Coma Scale < 13 or seizure (severe hypoglycaemia / hyponatraemia)  
  - NEWS2 ≥ 7 or lactate > 4 mmol/L despite initial treatment  
  - Failing to tolerate oral steroids after 24h with ongoing vomiting  
  - Persistent hyperkalaemia (K > 6.0) or hyponatraemia < 120 despite steroids & fluids  
  - Suspected acute Addisonian crisis with any haemodynamic instability – escalate to HDU/ICU for central line and inotropes if needed.

rawMarkdown:
## Addison's Disease
**One-liner:** Primary adrenal insufficiency; autoimmune destruction most common; causes cortisol & aldosterone deficiency; life-threatening if crisis missed.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Adrenal crisis (hypovolaemic shock, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia)**  
  *Single finding:* Hyperpigmentation + postural hypotension + U&Es showing Na < 130, K > 5.5 in a shocked patient.
- **Sepsis (precipitant or mimic of crisis)**  
  *Single finding:* Fever + raised CRP/procalcitonin – but if in shock with pigmentation, treat for crisis first.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Gradual onset fatigue, weakness, weight loss  
  - Anorexia, nausea, vomiting, diffuse abdominal pain  
  - Craving salt  
  - Dizziness on standing, syncope  
  - Reduced libido, loss of body hair (women)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Hyperpigmentation (buccal mucosa, palmar creases, scars, extensor surfaces)  
  - Postural hypotension (systolic drop ≥20 mmHg)  
  - Signs of volume depletion (tachycardia, dry mucous membranes)  
  - Rarely: vitiligo, goitre (associated autoimmune disease)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Other causes of hyponatraemia** (SIADH, hypothyroidism, heart failure) – no hyperkalaemia or pigmentation  
- **Secondary adrenal insufficiency** (pituitary disease, glucocorticoid withdrawal) – no hyperpigmentation, normal aldosterone (renin normal), Na may be low but K normal  
- **Chronic fatigue syndrome / depression** – no electrolyte abnormalities, normal cortisol  
- **Anorexia nervosa** – no hyperpigmentation, low BP but hyperkalaemia rare  
- **Acute abdomen (e.g. perforation, pancreatitis)** – localised tenderness, no pigmentation, imaging + metyrapone test negative  
- **Sepsis alone** – inflammatory markers high, but U&Es usually normal unless AKI  
- **Splitter:** Hyperkalaemia + hyponatraemia + hyperpigmentation → primary Addison’s, not secondary

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**  
  - Lying/standing BP (postural drop)  
  - ECG: look for hyperkalaemia (peaked T waves, widened QRS)  
  - Capillary glucose: hypoglycaemia common  
- **Bloods:**  
  - U&Es, glucose, cortisol (random – low < 100 nmol/L highly suggestive; if > 500, excludes)  
  - ACTH (elevated in primary)  
  - Renin / aldosterone (low aldosterone, high renin)  
  - Autoantibodies (21-hydroxylase antibodies)  
  - FBC, CRP, blood cultures (if sepsis suspected)  
  - Thyroid function, coeliac screen (associated autoimmune)  
- **Imaging:**  
  - CT adrenals (to exclude haemorrhage, metastases, TB) if no clear autoimmune cause  
  - MRI pituitary if secondary suspected  
- **Special tests:**  
  - **Short Synacthen test** – give 250 mcg tetracosactide IV; measure cortisol at 0 and 30 min.  
  - **Diagnostic criteria:** Peak cortisol < 550 nmol/L (or < 500 depending on lab) = adrenal insufficiency  
- **Never-miss test:**  
  - **Blood for cortisol and ACTH BEFORE giving hydrocortisone if patient stable enough** – but if critically ill (shock), treat immediately and send a baseline cortisol sample (result may still be helpful). Do NOT withhold steroids for test.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If adrenal crisis (shock, severe hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia):  
    - IV hydrocortisone 100 mg bolus stat  
    - IV 0.9% saline 1 L over 30-60 min, repeat as needed (total up to 2-3 L in first 2-3 hours)  
    - Dextrose 50 mL of 50% (25 g) IV if hypoglycaemic  
    - Correct hyperkalaemia cautiously (rehydration + steroids usually enough; avoid insulin unless ECG changes)  
  - Treat underlying cause (e.g. antibiotics for sepsis)  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Continue IV hydrocortisone 100 mg IV q6h (or 50 mg q6h) for 24-72 h, tapering as clinical condition improves  
  - Maintain IV 0.9% saline with 5% dextrose if needed, guided by U&Es and fluid balance  
  - Monitor: NEWS2 hourly initially, U&Es + glucose 6-hourly, strict fluid balance  
  - Transition to oral when tolerating diet and haemodynamically stable  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Oral hydrocortisone: typical total 15-25 mg daily in divided doses (e.g. 10 mg on waking, 5 mg early afternoon, 5 mg early evening) – *check local policy*  
  - Fludrocortisone: 50-200 mcg once daily (mineralocorticoid replacement) – start 100 mcg, adjust based on U&Es and postural BP  
  - Sick day rules: double or triple oral hydrocortisone dose during illness; provide IM/SC hydrocortisone emergency kit for vomiting  
  - MedicAlert bracelet, emergency card, annual endocrinology review  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline ± 5% dextrose, rate based on U&Es – aim for euvolemia  
  - VTE prophylaxis: has been on steroids? Consider LMWH if no contraindications  
  - Diet: normal, encourage oral intake; if NBM for procedure, ensure IV hydrocortisone cover  
  - Observations: NEWS2, lying/standing BP, daily weights, U&Es daily until stable

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**  
  - Hydrocortisone (IV): 100 mg bolus, then 100 mg q6h (taper as per local protocol)  
  - Hydrocortisone (oral): 15-25 mg total daily in 3 divided doses  
  - Fludrocortisone (oral): 50-200 mcg once daily  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Prednisolone (oral) 3-5 mg once daily – less preferred for mineralocorticoid effect  
  - Dexamethasone (IV/IM) if hydrocortisone unavailable (do not use for short Synacthen)  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Enzyme inducers (rifampicin, phenytoin, carbamazepine) accelerate hydrocortisone metabolism – increase dose  
  - Caution in peptic ulcer disease, diabetes, osteoporosis  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - No major DILI; adjust fludrocortisone if renal impairment (salt/water retention risk)  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Failing to give IV hydrocortisone in suspected adrenal crisis** – do not wait for test results.  
  - Missed stress dosing during intercurrent illness (e.g. surgery, infection) → precipitates crisis.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Adrenal crisis (hypotension, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia, cardiac arrhythmia)  
  - Vomiting → inability to take oral steroids → crisis cycle  
  - Hyperkalaemia-induced bradycardia or arrest  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Osteoporosis (long-term supraphysiologic steroids) – DXA scan if ongoing treatment years  
  - Autoimmune comorbidities: hypothyroidism, vitiligo, type 1 diabetes, pernicious anaemia, coeliac disease  
  - Adrenal crises with stress (50% of patients will have at least one crisis in 10 years)

### Prognosis
- Near-normal life expectancy with appropriate replacement and sick-day management  
- Mortality from adrenal crisis is ~6-15% per crisis episode if untreated; most deaths preventable with education and emergency kits

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Give a MedicAlert bracelet and emergency card** – patient must carry injectable hydrocortisone and written crisis plan.  
- **Check for other autoimmune diseases** – screen thyroid function, coeliac serology, and perform annual review for vitiligo, diabetes.  
- **Teach sick day rules explicitly** – double or triple oral dose for fever, vomiting, infection; go to ED if vomiting persists.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not delay steroids for test results** if patient is hypotensive or has typical biochemistry.  
- **Do not stop steroids abruptly** – taper over days when switching from IV to oral.  
- **Do not underestimate stress dose requirements** – cover procedures (minor surgery: 50-100 mg IV, major: 100 mg q6h) – *check local policy for dosing.*

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Vomiting or diarrhoea with inability to take oral steroids  
  - Severe weakness, dizziness, abdominal pain, confusion  
  - Any injury, surgery, or fever ≥38°C without contact with endocrinology team  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Endocrinology clinic within 2-4 weeks of new diagnosis; routine annual review  
  - GP for monitoring of U&Es, blood pressure, BP lying/standing

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU / crash team if:**  
  - Systolic BP < 90 mmHg despite 2 L IV fluid challenge  
  - Glasgow Coma Scale < 13 or seizure (severe hypoglycaemia / hyponatraemia)  
  - NEWS2 ≥ 7 or lactate > 4 mmol/L despite initial treatment  
  - Failing to tolerate oral steroids after 24h with ongoing vomiting  
  - Persistent hyperkalaemia (K > 6.0) or hyponatraemia < 120 despite steroids & fluids  
  - Suspected acute Addisonian crisis with any haemodynamic instability – escalate to HDU/ICU for central line and inotropes if needed.
